comment.body:
Based on these factors, the risk of another Yen Carry Trade significantly disrupting the US stock market in the immediate term is moderate. While the BOJ’s rate hike and tariff-driven volatility have diminished the carry trade’s appeal, the potential for further unwinding persists under certain conditions:
Key Triggers: Continued yen appreciation, persistent weakness in US tech stocks, or worsening global economic uncertainty could lead to additional selling pressure on US equities.
Mitigating Factors: The BOJ’s cautious stance and the partial unwinding already observed (e.g., the August 5 sell-off) suggest that the most severe disruptions may have already occurred.
Recommendations for Monitoring
Investors should keep a close eye on:
The yen’s exchange rate trajectory against the dollar.
BOJ policy announcements regarding interest rates and market interventions.
The performance of US tech stocks, as a proxy for high-yielding assets tied to the carry trade.
In summary, while the immediate risk is not extreme, the interplay of tariff impacts, yen strength, and market volatility means the US stock market remains vulnerable to residual effects of the Yen Carry Trade in the short term.

comment.body:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trading_curb
Read it. 
This is what I refer to at the end of my daily post.
It is in effect. Not every environment is good for intraday reversion trading.
We made reversion tradings when there was no black swan risk and left-tail risk, but steady suppressed intraday vol. 
This is not the same regime. Yet. 
(I may sell vol here betting on liquidity hunt... not decided yet)

But isn't it insane how you came here, we longed Feb, shorted the Feb OpEx (despite everybody called supportive feb opex), shorted the market all the way down, while played for intraday reversion tradings... longed into March OpEx, played the bulltrap while built put positions, and now here we are?

We never been fooled by the market :)))
